definition: Firms retaining excess workers (labor hoarding) creates fragility with potential nonlinear cliff risk rather than gradual cooling. Stable payrolls may be followed by sudden decompression if demand crosses a threshold. JOLTS signature: low layoffs ~1.0% alongside declining hires rate. Margin trigger estimated at ~8-9% S&P 500 net margin vs. current ~11-12%.
evidence: JOLTS data pattern: low layoffs ~1.0% alongside declining hires rate
Margin threshold trigger at ~8-9% S&P 500 net margin vs. current ~11-12%
Six agents converge on the mechanism
Labor hoarding is inferred from noisy productivity data, not directly measured
open_questions: Is hoarding inferred from noisy productivity data reliable?
If demand re-accelerates, does hoarded labor simply become productive, dissipating the risk?
Does survivorship bias (outcome-dependent relabeling of 'foresight' vs 'over-hiring') contaminate the evidence base?
How does maturity wall refinancing pressure interact with hoarding break timing?
